title: Open-air perovskite fabrication - Nature Energy
text:
Access through your institution

Buy or subscribe

Efforts to commercialize perovskite solar materials can follow divergent strategies. Improving the power conversion and degradation performance, developing perovskite–silicon tandem devices, or reducing manufacturing costs are all possible routes. Now, Austin Flick and Reinhold Dauskardt at Stanford University report, based on technoeconomic analysis, that the cost-reduction route may offer the most viable route to commercialization of perovskite solar modules. In particular, they show that fabrication methods that can take place in air — as opposed to conventional vacuum or inert conditions — could enable modules with costs far below those of devices produced via the other routes.The researchers built a model for producing perovskite photovoltaic devices that considered material inputs, the manufacturing steps required, and the performance and lifetime of the resulting devices. By linking the device efficiency, operational lifetime and manufacturing costs, the model shows that perovskite devices do not need to match silicon devices in lifetime in order to outperform them on overall electricity cost over a 30-year project horizon. Flick and Dauskardt estimate that an open-air perovskite production facility producing 10 GW of solar panels would have 77% lower costs than one of the same scale using conventional fabrication techniques. Consequently, the resulting devices can be replaced as they fail over the course of 30 years and still cumulatively cost less than devices that last the full 30 years but need highly controlled fabrication facilities. The findings suggest that the reduction of production costs may be more important than device lifetime or performance metrics for commercial viability.
